content:
# Genesis Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6v–7r | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_011_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_011_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 7–8 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

## The Tower of Babel

**1** At that time the whole earth spoke one language — the same words, the same speech.

**2** As people pushed eastward, they found a broad plain in the land of Shinar and settled there.

**3** They said to one another, "Come — let's fire some bricks." And they used brick for stone and tar for mortar.

**4** Then they said, "Come — let's build ourselves a city, with a tower that reaches the sky. We'll make a name for ourselves, so we won't be scattered across the face of the whole earth."

**5** The LORD came down to see the city and the tower that the humans were building.

**6** The LORD said, "Look — they are one people with one language, and this is only the beginning of what they will do. Nothing they set out to do will be beyond them now.

**7** "Come — let us go down and scramble their language there, so they can't understand each other."

**8** So the LORD scattered them from there across the face of the whole earth, and they stopped building the city.

**9** That is why it was named Babel — because there the LORD scrambled the language of the whole earth, and from there the LORD scattered them across the face of the whole earth.

---

## The Line of Shem

**10** These are the generations of Shem.

When Shem was 100 years old, he fathered Arphaxad — two years after the flood. **11** And after he fathered Arphaxad, Shem lived another 500 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**12** When Arphaxad was 35 years old, he fathered Shelah. **13** And after he fathered Shelah, Arphaxad lived another 403 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**14** When Shelah was 30 years old, he fathered Eber. **15** And after he fathered Eber, Shelah lived another 403 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**16** When Eber was 34 years old, he fathered Peleg. **17** And after he fathered Peleg, Eber lived another 430 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**18** When Peleg was 30 years old, he fathered Reu. **19** And after he fathered Reu, Peleg lived another 209 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**20** When Reu was 32 years old, he fathered Serug. **21** And after he fathered Serug, Reu lived another 207 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**22** When Serug was 30 years old, he fathered Nahor. **23** And after he fathered Nahor, Serug lived another 200 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**24** When Nahor was 29 years old, he fathered Terah. **25** And after he fathered Terah, Nahor lived another 119 years, and had other sons and daughters.

**26** After Terah had lived 70 years, he fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran.

---

## The Line of Terah

**27** These are the generations of Terah.

Terah fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran. And Haran fathered Lot. **28** Haran died before his father Terah, in the land of his birth — in Ur of the Chaldeans.

**29** Abram and Nahor both married. Abram's wife was named Sarai, and Nahor's wife was named Milcah — she was the daughter of Haran, who was the father of both Milcah and Iscah. **30** But Sarai was barren; she had no child.

**31** Terah took his son Abram and his grandson Lot — Haran's son — and his daughter-in-law Sarai, Abram's wife, and he set out with them from Ur of the Chaldeans to travel to the land of Canaan. But when they reached Haran, they stopped and settled there.

**32** The days of Terah came to 205 years, and Terah died in Haran.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The architecture of Genesis 11 — a hinge between worlds

Genesis 11 does something Genesis has not yet done: it makes you feel the ground shift under your feet. The chapter opens on a unified humanity building toward heaven and closes on a single family, stopped at a city called Haran, their destination unreached, their matriarch barren. Between the tower's collapse and Abram's emergence, the genealogy counts down like a clock running slow — lifespans shrinking, the world narrowing from all the nations to one man, one woman, one impossible promise.

The chapter is the hinge of the primeval history. Everything before it (Genesis 1–10) has been universal: creation, flood, table of nations. Everything after it will be particular: the family of Abraham, the story of Israel. Genesis 11 is where the universal story fractures — deliberately, by divine design — into a single thread.

---

### Verse 1 — "one lip" and the gift that became a problem

The Hebrew is arrestingly spare: **שָׂפָה אֶחָת וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים** (*safah 'echat udvarim 'achadim*) — literally "one lip and words of one-ness." The word *safah* (lip, language) is the same word used in the curiously intimate image of God's face: *lifnei pene* — the mouth of the earth. Language is embodied, physical. One lip: one mouth, one breath, one way of shaping sound into meaning.

The phrase *devarim 'achadim* (translated "the same words," literally "words of one-ness" or "unified words") is subtler. It may mean unified vocabulary, or shared purpose, or both — language as social bond, as coordination technology, as the thing that lets human beings act together at scale. This one language is not presented as a problem. It is the condition of everything that follows. The capacity to say *Come, let us* — and have everyone understand — is precisely what enables Babel. The gift precedes the catastrophe.

---

### Verses 3–4 — "Come, let us" / "Come, let us"

One of the most carefully constructed ironies in all of Genesis. The humans say **הָבָה נִלְבְּנָה לְבֵנִים** — "Come, let us make bricks" (v. 3) — and **הָבָה נִבְנֶה לָנוּ עִיר** — "Come, let us build for ourselves a city" (v. 4). Then God says **הָבָה נֵרְדָה** — "Come, let us go down" (v. 7). The structural echo is deliberate: the humans' "let us" is mimicked by the divine "let us." What the humans build in council, God dismantles in council. The story is a mirror: human ambition meets divine response in the same grammatical form.

The divine plural ("let us") here echoes the council of 1:26 ("Let us make humanity") and 3:22 ("the human has become like one of us"). This is the heavenly assembly at work — or, at minimum, a reminder that God does not act alone in the Hebrew imagination, but speaks in a voice that includes and exceeds the singular. The irony cuts both ways: the humans thought "let us" was the syntax of transcendence, the grammar of gods. And they were right. Which is exactly why God answered in kind.

---

### Verse 4 — a name, a tower, and the fear of scattering

"Let us make a name for ourselves" — **וְנַעֲשֶׂה לָּנוּ שֵׁם** (*venaaseh lanu shem*). The word *shem* means both "name" and "renown." To make a name is to be remembered, to persist, to outrun death through fame. Every empire, every monument, every conquering civilization has wanted this. The text is not naively reporting a plan; it is diagnosing a drive. The drive to be remembered, to be great, to leave a mark — and the specific fear driving it: "lest we be scattered" (**פֶּן נָפוּץ** *pen nafuts*). They don't want to spread out. They want to stay together, to concentrate power, to build the thing that will hold everything in place.

The tower with its "top in the heavens" (**רֹאשׁוֹ בַשָּׁמַיִם** *ro'sho vashamayim*) is a *ziggurat* — a Mesopotamian stepped temple-tower whose summit was conceived as a meeting point between heaven and earth, the throne-room of a god. Shinar is Sumer. These people are building a ziggurat. The text is not presenting this as exotic foreign religion; it is presenting it as the universal human impulse dressed in its grandest architectural form: reach heaven, plant a flag, be remembered.

---

### Verse 5 — the LORD came *down*

**וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה לִרְאֹת** (*vayyered YHWH lir'ot*) — "The LORD came *down* to see." The irony is breathtaking, and entirely intentional. The tower with its top in the heavens — and the LORD has to *come down* to even find it. All that human effort to reach up, and from where God stands, it is invisible without stooping. The anthropomorphism is not naive; it is satirical. The narrator is making a theological point through comic proportion: what humanity experiences as heaven-storming ambition, God experiences as something requiring a closer look.

The verb *lir'ot* — "to see" — echoes God's *seeing* throughout Genesis: God saw the light (1:4), God saw everything he made (1:31), God saw the wickedness of humanity (6:5). Divine seeing is always consequential in this narrative. When God looks, something happens.

---

### Verse 7 — balal: the sound of confusion

"Let us go down and *scramble* their language." The Hebrew verb is **בָּלַל** (*balal*) — to mix, to confuse, to jumble. And this is the pun the whole story has been building toward. The city is called **בָּבֶל** (*Babel*) — and the text explains the name as derived from *balal*: Babel because God *balal*-ed the speech there. The name sounds like confusion.

This is folk etymology — historically, "Babel" is Akkadian *Bab-ili*, "Gate of God," the name the Babylonians gave their own city, meaning the very opposite of confusion. But the Hebrew author is playing against that meaning. The great city that called itself the Gate of God — where heaven and earth touched, where the gods descended — turns out to be, in Israel's telling, the place of confusion, the site of the great scrambling. "Gate of God" becomes "babble." The most magnificent city in the ancient world, seen from outside, as cacophony.

---

### Verses 10–26 — the genealogy that counts down

The genealogy of Shem to Abram is structured to be felt. Run the numbers:

| Name | Age at first son | Years after | Total |
|------|-----------------|-------------|-------|
| Shem | 100 | 500 | 600 |
| Arphaxad | 35 | 403 | 438 |
| Shelah | 30 | 403 | 433 |
| Eber | 34 | 430 | 464 |
| Peleg | 30 | 209 | 239 |
| Reu | 32 | 207 | 239 |
| Serug | 30 | 200 | 230 |
| Nahor | 29 | 119 | 148 |
| Terah | — | — | 205 |

The lifespans fall. From Noah's 950, to Shem's 600, to the low 200s by Peleg's generation, to Nahor's 148, to Terah's 205. The world is aging — or rather, the extraordinary longevity of the primeval era is being left behind as the story moves toward ordinary human time. By the time we reach Abraham, people will live roughly the length of a long human life. The genealogy is a transition not just of family line but of *mode of existence*: from mythic time to historical time.

The LXX (Codex Vaticanus, page 7) inserts a generation here: Cainan, son of Arphaxad, father of Shelah — consistent with the LXX throughout and cited in Luke 3:36. The PNEUMA translation follows the Masoretic Text, which moves directly from Arphaxad to Shelah.

---

### Verses 27–32 — Terah's line: arrival of the particular

The sixth *toledot* formula — "these are the generations of Terah" — makes Terah the nominal subject of the genealogy. But Terah is not the story. He is the frame for three sons — Abram, Nahor, Haran — and the figure who sets out for Canaan and doesn't make it.

Three narrative details in these six verses crack open the rest of Genesis:

**Haran dies young.** Verse 28 is the first death of a son in his father's lifetime in the genealogical record. Haran dies "before his father Terah," in Ur of the Chaldeans. He leaves behind a son (Lot) and a daughter (Milcah, who marries her uncle Nahor). Death is now inside the family — not just at the end of a long life but mid-generation, unexplained. It is the first note of grief.

**Sarai is barren.** Verse 30 delivers the sentence with brutal efficiency: **וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד** (*vattehiy Saray aqarah 'eyn lah valad*) — "Sarai was barren; she had no child." The doubling — "barren" and "had no child" — is not redundancy. It is emphasis. The word *'aqarah* (barren) is the same word used for the ancestral women who couldn't conceive: Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, the Shunammite woman. Barrenness in Genesis is not biological backstory. It is the theological condition from which miracle emerges. But at this moment in the narrative, it is simply stated as a fact. The reader does not yet know that this fact will become the engine of everything. The story of Israel will begin precisely here: with a woman who cannot bear a child, in a family that has set out for Canaan and stopped halfway.

**Terah stops short.** He sets out for Canaan — the land that will become the promised land — and settles in Haran instead. Why? The text doesn't say. He dies there, 205 years old, his journey unfinished. The promise that will come to Abram in chapter 12 is, in a sense, the resumption of a journey Terah began and couldn't complete. What the father started, the son will be called to finish.

---

### The name "Babel" — and why the story doesn't end there

It is worth pausing at what the Babel narrative does *not* say. It does not say the tower was evil. It does not call its builders wicked. It says they had one language and one purpose and that they built. The LORD's response — "nothing they set out to do will be beyond them now" (v. 6) — is not a condemnation. It reads almost like admiration, or at least a sober assessment of human capacity. The scattering is not punishment for sin so much as a limiting of a kind of consolidated power that, in the narrative's logic, would remove the condition under which human beings live as human beings: finite, diverse, dependent.

The Babel story is the counter-image to Pentecost (Acts 2), which the New Testament writers clearly understood. At Pentecost, the divided languages are not reunited into one language — everyone hears in their *own* tongue. Unity-in-diversity, not the erasure of difference. The reversal of Babel is not uniformity but mutual understanding across plurality. The Babel story sets up not just the genealogy of Abram but the entire biblical arc toward a redeemed humanity that does not need a tower because the distance between heaven and earth has been closed another way.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6v–7r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 7–8 (pa_0007, pa_0008).*

---

*— PNEUMA*
